Deep Learning · Remote Sensing · Spatial Downscaling

Precipitation Downscaling Using Deep Learning and Google Earth Engine

Study Area: Dhaka District, Bangladesh Period: January 2002 – December 2025 Resolution: 10 km → 1 km Author: Istiaque Ahamed Nabed Affiliation: Dept. of Disaster Science and Climate Resilience, University of Dhaka
GEE · xee · xarray NASA GPM IMERG MODIS (LST · NDVI · ET) TensorFlow / Keras MLP StandardScaler · sklearn Python · Google Colab

Overview

Precipitation data from satellite products such as NASA's GPM IMERG are available at coarse spatial resolutions (~10 km), which limits their utility for local-scale hydrological, agricultural, and urban flood risk assessments. This project develops a statistical downscaling framework that uses a fully connected deep learning model to enhance the spatial resolution of GPM IMERG monthly precipitation from ~10 km to ~1 km over Dhaka District, Bangladesh.

The approach leverages the fact that high-resolution ancillary variables — vegetation indices (NDVI, EVI), land surface temperature (LST), evapotranspiration (ET), land cover type, and topography — are available at finer spatial resolutions from MODIS and USGS. The neural network is trained on coarse-resolution collocated data and then applied to the high-resolution predictors to generate spatially detailed precipitation estimates.

GPM IMERG
(10 km)
→
MODIS + DEM
Predictors
→
MLP

1 km

Maps (2024)

Tutorial Credit

📺 Based on Tutorial

This project is adapted from Google Earth Engine Tutorial-176: Precipitation Downscaling using Deep Learning Modelling by Amir Hossein Ahrari

The tutorial has been adapted to Dhaka District, Bangladesh (2002–2025), extended with post-processing corrections for negative value artefacts and monthly overprediction, and annotated with detailed methodological commentary.

Data Sources

VariableGEE DatasetResolutionDescription
prNASA/GPM_L3/IMERG_MONTHLY_V07~10 kmMonthly precipitation (mm/hr) — target variable
ndvi, eviMODIS/061/MOD13Q1250 mVegetation indices (16-day → monthly mean)
temp_day, temp_nightMODIS/061/MOD11A21 kmLand surface temperature, day & night (8-day → monthly mean)
etMODIS/061/MOD16A2GF500 mEvapotranspiration (8-day gap-filled → monthly mean)
lcMODIS/061/MCD12Q1500 mIGBP land cover type (temporal mode over study period)
demUSGS/GTOPO30~1 kmDigital elevation model (static)

All dynamic variables were resampled to monthly temporal resolution using mean compositing within GEE. The full time series spans January 2002 – December 2025 over the Dhaka District bounding box (lon: 90.25°–90.75°E, lat: 23.65°–24.05°N).

Methodology

Environment Setup

1
Install xee — GEE–xarray Bridge

Install the xee package, which enables reading Google Earth Engine ImageCollection objects directly as xarray datasets using the Earth Engine backend. This eliminates the need to export data to Google Drive for array-based analysis.

!pip install --upgrade xee
Result
xee version 0.1.1 is already installed along with all required dependencies including xarray, earthengine-api, and dask.
2
Install geemap — Interactive GEE Mapping

Install geemap, an interactive Python mapping library built on the Google Earth Engine API, providing Jupyter-friendly tools for spatial visualization and ROI drawing.

!pip install --upgrade geemap
Result
geemap is already present in the environment from a prior installation.
3
Authenticate and Initialize Earth Engine

Authenticate the GEE session and initialize with the high-volume Earth Engine endpoint, which is recommended for large-scale spatiotemporal data extraction workflows.

import ee
ee.Authenticate()
ee.Initialize(
    opt_url='https://earthengine-highvolume.googleapis.com'
)
Result
GEE session authenticated and initialized successfully. The high-volume endpoint reduces rate-limiting when querying dense multi-variable image collections.

GEE Data Collection

4
Define Region of Interest — Dhaka District

Extract the Dhaka district boundary from the FAO GAUL 2015 Level-2 administrative dataset by filtering for Dhaka Division and Dhaka District. This polygon constrains all subsequent GEE computations to the study area.

roi = (
    ee.FeatureCollection("FAO/GAUL/2015/level2")
    .filter(ee.Filter.eq('ADM1_NAME', 'Dhaka'))   # Dhaka division
    .filter(ee.Filter.eq('ADM2_NAME', 'Dhaka'))   # Dhaka district
    .geometry()
)
Result
The Dhaka district boundary is successfully extracted as an ee.Geometry object and confirmed visually on the interactive map.
5
Build Multi-Variable Monthly ImageCollection (2002–2024)

Construct a monthly time series by aggregating six satellite datasets to a common monthly temporal resolution using mean compositing. All dynamic variables are combined as multi-band images with the static land cover and DEM appended at every time step.

time_start = ee.Date('2002')
time_end   = ee.Date('2025')
time_dif   = time_end.difference(time_start, 'month').round()
time_list  = ee.List.sequence(0, ee.Number(time_dif).subtract(1)).map(
    lambda x: time_start.advance(x, 'month')
)

def monthly(date_list, col):
    start_date = ee.Date(date_list)
    end_date   = start_date.advance(1, 'month')
    return col.filterDate(start_date, end_date).mean() \
              .set('system:time_start', start_date.millis())

pr      = ee.ImageCollection("NASA/GPM_L3/IMERG_MONTHLY_V07")
            .filterDate(time_start, time_end)
            .select(['precipitation'], ['pr'])
veg     = ee.ImageCollection("MODIS/061/MOD13Q1")  ...
temp    = ee.ImageCollection("MODIS/061/MOD11A2")   ...
et      = ee.ImageCollection("MODIS/061/MOD16A2GF") ...

# Combine into single multi-band collection
collection = veg_monthly.combine(temp_monthly)
                         .combine(et_monthly)
                         .combine(pr_monthly)
                         .map(lambda x: x.addBands(landcover).addBands(topo))
Result
A combined ee.ImageCollection is created with 276 monthly images (Jan 2002 – Dec 2025), each containing 8 bands: ndvi, evi, temp_day, temp_night, et, pr, lc, dem.

Coarse-Resolution (10 km) Grid

6
Load 10 km Dataset via xee

Define a 0.1° grid (~10 km) over the Dhaka District bounding box and open the GEE ImageCollection as an xarray.Dataset using the xee engine. This grid matches the native resolution of GPM IMERG precipitation and serves as the training domain.

min_lon, min_lat, max_lon, max_lat = 90.25, 23.65, 90.75, 24.05
scale_deg    = 0.1   # ~10 km
width        = int(np.ceil((max_lon - min_lon) / scale_deg))  # 5
height       = int(np.ceil((max_lat - min_lat) / scale_deg))  # 5
crs_transform = (scale_deg, 0, min_lon, 0, -scale_deg, max_lat)

ds10km = xr.open_dataset(
    collection, engine='ee',
    crs='EPSG:4326',
    crs_transform=crs_transform,
    shape_2d=(height, width),
)
ds10km = ds10km.rename({'y': 'lat', 'x': 'lon'})
Result
Grid shape confirmed: 5 × 5 (25 pixels, ~400 km²/pixel). Dataset dimensions: (time: 276, lat: 5, lon: 5) with total size ~223 KB. Spatial coordinates: lat ∈ [23.6°, 24.0°], lon ∈ [90.3°, 90.7°].
7
Convert to Tabular Format and Remove Missing Values

Sort chronologically, materialize the lazy GEE-backed arrays, and convert the xarray.Dataset to a pandas DataFrame indexed by (time, lat, lon). Rows with any NaN values are dropped to produce a clean training dataset.

ds10km   = ds10km.sortby('time') * 1
df10km   = ds10km.to_dataframe().dropna()
Result
DataFrame indexed by (time, lat, lon). Raw MODIS-scaled values observed (e.g., NDVI ~3,741; LST ~14,881 in instrument units). Precipitation (pr) in mm/hr. Multi-index structure confirms spatial-temporal panel data format.

Data Preprocessing

8
Standardize Predictor Variables (10 km)

Select the seven predictor features and apply Z-score standardization (zero mean, unit variance) using StandardScaler. Standardization is essential for neural network training stability — it ensures no single variable (e.g., raw LST values in the thousands) dominates gradient updates.

from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

x10km           = df10km[['ndvi','evi','temp_day','temp_night','et','lc','dem']]
scaler_x10km    = StandardScaler()
x10km_standard  = scaler_x10km.fit_transform(x10km)
Result
Standardized feature array computed. All 7 predictors are now zero-centered with unit variance. Mixed-sign values in the output confirm meaningful variance across spatial-temporal observations.
9
Standardize Target Variable (10 km)

Extract monthly precipitation (pr) as the regression target and apply Z-score standardization. Standardizing the target avoids gradient scaling issues during backpropagation and enables symmetric error penalization.

y10km          = df10km['pr']
scaler_y10km   = StandardScaler()
y10km_standard = scaler_y10km.fit_transform(y10km.values.reshape(-1,1))
Result
Standardized precipitation returned as a float32 column vector. Predominantly negative values indicate most observations fall below the long-term mean — consistent with dry-season months dominating the 23-year record.
10
Train-Test Split (80/20)

Split the standardized 10 km dataset into training (80%) and test (20%) subsets using a fixed random seed for reproducibility.

from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

x_train, x_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    x10km_standard, y10km_standard,
    test_size=0.2, random_state=42
)
Result
Dataset split into training and held-out test sets. Fixed random_state=42 ensures reproducible partitioning across runs.

Deep Learning Model

11
Define and Train the MLP Regression Model

Build a fully connected feedforward neural network (Multi-Layer Perceptron) for precipitation regression using the Keras Sequential API. The architecture progressively reduces dimensionality from 64 to 1 with ReLU activations.

LayerUnitsActivation
Input7 features—
Dense 164ReLU
Dense 232ReLU
Dense 316ReLU
Output1Linear
 
from tensorflow.keras.models import Sequential
from tensorflow.keras.layers import Dense

model = Sequential([
    Dense(64, activation='relu', input_dim=x10km.shape[1]),
    Dense(32, activation='relu'),
    Dense(16, activation='relu'),
    Dense(1)
])
model.compile(optimizer='adam', loss='mse', metrics=['mae'])

model.fit(
    x_train, y_train,
    batch_size=8, epochs=50,
    validation_split=0.2, verbose=1
)
model_evaluation = model.evaluate(x_test, y_test)
Training Results — 50 Epochs
0.3946
Train MSE (Epoch 1)
0.1972
Train MSE (Epoch 50)
0.2458
Val MSE (Epoch 50)
0.3275
Val MAE (Epoch 50)

Training loss decreased by ~50% over 50 epochs, indicating effective learning from the coarse-resolution predictors. Validation loss stabilized around 0.24–0.25, confirming reasonable generalization without severe overfitting. Test set evaluation yielded MSE ≈ 0.25, MAE ≈ 0.33 (standardized units).

1 km Downscaling

12
Load High-Resolution (1 km) Predictor Grid

Redefine the spatial grid at 0.01° resolution (~1 km) over the same Dhaka District bounding box. The same GEE ImageCollection now delivers MODIS predictors at their native fine resolution — this becomes the downscaling target domain where the trained model is applied.

scale_deg     = 0.01   # ~1 km
width         = int(np.ceil((max_lon - min_lon) / scale_deg))  # 50
height        = int(np.ceil((max_lat - min_lat) / scale_deg))  # 41
crs_transform = (scale_deg, 0, min_lon, 0, -scale_deg, max_lat)

ds1km = xr.open_dataset(
    collection, engine='ee',
    crs='EPSG:4326',
    crs_transform=crs_transform,
    shape_2d=(height, width),
)
Result
High-resolution grid confirmed: 41 × 50 (2,050 pixels per time step), compared to 5 × 5 at 10 km — an 82× increase in spatial detail. The affine transform (0.01, 0, 90.25, 0, -0.01, 24.05) is set correctly.
13
Apply Model and Generate 1 km Precipitation Estimates

Standardize the 1 km predictor features using a new StandardScaler fitted on the high-resolution data. Apply the trained model to generate standardized precipitation predictions at 1 km, then inverse-transform back to physical units (mm/hr). Predictions are stored as pr1km alongside the original coarse pr in an xarray.Dataset.

x1km          = df1km[['ndvi','evi','temp_day','temp_night','et','lc','dem']]
scaler_x1km   = StandardScaler()
x1km_standard = scaler_x1km.fit_transform(x1km)

scaler_y1km   = StandardScaler()
y1km_standard = scaler_y1km.fit_transform(y1km.values.reshape(-1,1))

df1km['pr1km'] = scaler_y1km.inverse_transform(
    model.predict(x1km_standard)
)
result = df1km[['pr','pr1km']].to_xarray().sortby(['time','lat','lon'])
Result
Model inference completed across 12,746 batches in ~25 seconds covering the full 1 km spatial-temporal grid (276 months × 2,050 pixels). The pr1km column now contains spatially downscaled precipitation estimates at ~1 km resolution for the entire 2002–2024 period.

Post-Processing

14
Correct Artefacts in Downscaled Precipitation

Initial inspection of the downscaled maps revealed two artefacts common in statistical downscaling: (1) physically impossible negative precipitation values (neural networks can predict below zero), and (2) extreme overprediction in October. Two targeted post-processing fixes are applied:

  • Clip negative values: All predicted values below zero are set to zero.
  • Monthly quantile capping: For each calendar month, predictions are capped at the 99th percentile of observed GPM precipitation for that month. This removes extreme outliers while preserving the seasonal distribution shape.
# Fix 1: Clip negative values
df1km['pr1km'] = df1km['pr1km'].clip(lower=0)

# Fix 2: Cap by monthly 99th percentile of observed precipitation
df1km = df1km.reset_index()
df1km['month'] = pd.to_datetime(df1km['time']).dt.month

def cap_by_month(group):
    month  = group['month'].iloc[0]
    pr_cap = df1km[df1km['month'] == month]['pr'].quantile(0.99)
    group['pr1km'] = group['pr1km'].clip(upper=pr_cap)
    return group

df1km = df1km.groupby('month', group_keys=False).apply(cap_by_month)

# Rebuild xarray
df1km  = df1km.set_index(['time', 'lat', 'lon'])
result = df1km[['pr','pr1km']].to_xarray().sortby(['time','lat','lon'])
Result
After post-processing: all negative values eliminated; October overprediction suppressed to physically plausible range. The corrected pr1km fields now exhibit spatially coherent monsoon seasonality — dry in January–February, peak in June–September — consistent with the coarse GPM reference.

Step 24 Output — Corrected GPM pr & pr1km (After Post-Processing)

Figures 3 and 4 show the final corrected outputs. The post-processed pr1km (Figure 4) retains the monsoon seasonality of the reference GPM product (Figure 3) while adding ~1 km spatial detail driven by the MODIS predictor fields — without physically implausible negative values or extreme outliers.

Figure 3. Original GPM IMERG precipitation (pr) for 2024, shown again for direct visual comparison with corrected downscaled output (Figure 4). Colormap: viridis.

Figure 4. Corrected 1 km downscaled precipitation (pr1km) after post-processing (negative clipping + monthly 99th-percentile capping), 2024. Spatial heterogeneity from MODIS predictors is now visible at ~1 km resolution. Colormap: viridis.

Results

Initial vs. Corrected Downscaled Maps (2024)

Visualization Note: Facet grid maps were generated using xarray.plot() with col='time' and col_wrap=6 for monthly panels (January–December 2024). Both original GPM pr and downscaled pr1km fields are shown at 1900 × 600 px with 13 panels per figure. Final corrected maps use the viridis colormap for perceptually uniform representation.

Step 23 Output — Original GPM pr & Initial pr1km (Before Post-Processing)

The original coarse-resolution GPM pr maps (Figure 1) show spatially smooth precipitation with clear monsoon seasonality: near-zero in January–February, rising to peak values in June–August. The initial downscaled pr1km (Figure 2) captures spatial heterogeneity but exhibits artefacts — negative values (blue pixels) and extreme overprediction in October — requiring post-processing correction.

Figure 1. Original GPM IMERG coarse-resolution precipitation (pr) for Dhaka District, 2024 — monthly facet maps. Units: mm/hr. Colormap: viridis.

Figure 2. Initial downscaled precipitation (pr1km) before post-processing, 2024 — diverging colormap (red = overprediction above GPM, blue = negative values). Artefacts visible in October and western pixels.

Model Performance Summary

MetricTraining SetValidation SetTest Set
MSE (standardized)0.1970.246~0.25
MAE (standardized)0.3050.328~0.33
Loss reduction~50% over 50 epochs (0.3946 → 0.1972)

The gap between training and validation loss (0.197 vs 0.246) indicates mild overfitting, typical for statistical downscaling models trained on small spatial grids (25 coarse pixels). Future work could incorporate dropout regularization, larger training domains, or ensemble approaches.

Key Finding: The deep learning downscaling framework successfully enhances GPM IMERG precipitation from ~10 km to ~1 km resolution over Dhaka District while preserving observed monsoon seasonality. Post-processing corrections are essential to remove physically implausible negative values and extreme monthly overprediction artefacts inherent in unconstrained neural network regression.

References

Ahrari, A. (2025). Google Earth Engine Tutorial-176: Precipitation Downscaling using Deep Learning Modelling

Huffman, G.J., et al. (2019). NASA Global Precipitation Measurement (GPM) Integrated Multi-satellitE Retrievals for GPM (IMERG). Algorithm Theoretical Basis Document, Version 06.

Wan, Z., Hook, S., & Hulley, G. (2021). MOD11A2 MODIS/Terra Land Surface Temperature/Emissivity 8-Day L3 Global 1km SIN Grid V061. NASA EOSDIS LPDAAC. https://doi.org/10.5067/MODIS/MOD11A2.061

Didan, K. (2021). MODIS/Terra Vegetation Indices 16-Day L3 Global 250m SIN Grid V061 (MOD13Q1). NASA EOSDIS LPDAAC. https://doi.org/10.5067/MODIS/MOD13Q1.061

Running, S., Mu, Q., & Zhao, M. (2021). MOD16A2GF MODIS/Terra Net Evapotranspiration Gap-Filled 8-Day L4 Global 500m SIN Grid V061. NASA EOSDIS LPDAAC. https://doi.org/10.5067/MODIS/MOD16A2GF.061

Friedl, M. & Sulla-Menashe, D. (2022). MODIS/Terra+Aqua Land Cover Type Yearly L3 Global 500m SIN Grid V061 (MCD12Q1). NASA EOSDIS LPDAAC. https://doi.org/10.5067/MODIS/MCD12Q1.061

Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO). (2015). Global Administrative Unit Layers (GAUL) 2015 — Level 2. FAO GeoNetwork.
